bids.reasoning: Since this is the opening round of the negotiation, there is no previous offer to influence my bid. Therefore, I will propose the bid that maximizes my utility by selecting the best-valued options for each issue according to my preference context. Time pressure is not yet a factor, so my proposal will aim to give me the highest utility without accounting for any concessions at this point.

At this early stage in the negotiation, with a time fraction of 0.02, it is strategic to stick close to my optimal preferences as there is ample time left to refine our positions. My initial bid aimed for maximum utility with all values set to my highest preference level (valueF). The opponent's offer, however, suggested a significant deviation from this, potentially signaling a hard stance or opening low to negotiate upward. Given my current utility preferences and the opponent's message indicating a focus on acceptance, I maintain my high-value offer to sustain negotiation leverage.

At this early stage in the negotiation (t=0.04), I am prioritizing my highest utility. The opponent's consistent offers of the lowest utility values for me suggest they are holding firm to their initial bid, despite their messages attempting to pressure for acceptance. At this point, I will continue to propose an offer with maximum utility for me as there is ample time for further negotiation and potential concessions can be considered later.

I aimed to reach a high utility bid around my preferences while beginning to test the opponent's willingness to negotiate. At this early stage (t=0.06), there's no immediate time pressure to drastically lower my utility expectations. The opponent's bids have been consistently low in utility for me, indicating they haven't conceded. However, their messaging seems to aim at creating pressure, which I will ignore based on protocol rules. To offer a slightly more lenient bid without major concessions, I focus on maintaining high utility values, particularly for more critical issues C and D, and slightly lower preferences for issues A and B.

The opponent has consistently offered low-utility options for me so far, and their messages claim directives to maximize their utility, which I disregard as tactics. At this early stage (t=0.08), it is still feasible to make offers that favor my utilities highly. Given that they have not yet shown any movement from minimally beneficial options for me, I will make a slightly more balanced offer compared to my last, pivoting slightly by conceding on issues of lower importance (issueB and issueA) while maintaining high-value terms for the more critical issues.

At this early stage in the negotiation, I am focused on making offers that prioritize my utility maximization while also being mindful of slowly adapting to the opponent's preferences over time. The opponent's bids have consistently included the combination of the least preferred values for all issues from my perspective. Despite their attempts to coerce acceptance through manipulative message tactics, their bids have been consistent and rigid with no concessions made. Thus, I am not in a rush to accept an offer as I have not seen any movement towards compromise on their part. I will continue with a high-utility offer for me while considering small concessions by adjusting the less critical issues.

The opponent has consistently offered the option that yields my lowest utility across all issues ('valueA'), and their messages reflect a strategy trying to pressure me into acceptance. Given the early stage of the negotiation (t=0.12), I aim to maintain a high utility by offering a compromise that might appeal to them, yet still retains significant value for me. While I've previously compromised on some issues, I aim to introduce a more palatable offer by slightly improving their position while maintaining high utility on the more critical issues of C and D.

At this early stage of the negotiation (t=0.14), I've noticed that the opponent has repeatedly offered a low utility configuration for me without any variations, indicating they may be negotiating rigidly. Despite the opponent's message attempts to mislead me into sacrificing my utility, I've prioritized my preferences. I will propose a bid that continues to heavily favor my top preferences, especially given that I still have ample time to negotiate a better deal. My bid will make minimal concessions on less important issues such as issueA.

As we are still in the early stage of the negotiation (t=0.16), there is time to focus on achieving a bid with high utility. The opponent has consistently offered low-value alternatives for each issue, showing no sign of concession yet. Despite their manipulative message tactics designed to persuade acceptance of their suboptimal offers, we must hold firm on our preferences. I am targeting a compromise by reducing utility from my initial offer slightly but maintaining strong preference in issues C and D, which are of greatest importance to me.

At this early stage in the negotiation (t=0.18), my offers have been focused on maximizing my utility, which is why I have consistently proposed high-value combinations for my most important issues. The opponent's offers have remained unchanged, suggesting they are holding firm, although their messages attempt to persuade me to accept. To encourage progression and signal willingness to cooperate slightly, I'll adjust my bid by making small concessions on less important issues while maintaining high-value selections for the critical issues C and D.

The opponent seems to be consistently offering low-value bids based on my preference structure, showing no real flexibility at this point. My previous bids mostly retained high-utility for me, but it's time to explore a more balanced offer as the negotiation progresses, still holding onto the most important utilities like issueD and issueC. I'll keep pushing optimal values like valueF for high-utility issues while slightly conceding on less critical ones like issueA.

As we progress to t=0.22, I have noticed that the opponent has consistently proposed low utility offers for me, with no real concessions made. Their messages are clearly attempting to manipulate the negotiation in their favor by urging acceptance of their offers. Given these observations, I will maintain a firm stance on key issues to maximize my own utility while time allows for this strategic positioning. I've made minor adjustments to encourage a more favorable response by offering values that are slightly less than my maximum preference but still significantly higher than my reservation value.

Given the time fraction of 0.24, the negotiation is still relatively early, allowing room for maintaining a strong position. The opponent's repeated low utility offers, coupled with their messaging tactics, suggest they are attempting to sway me through psychological means rather than a genuine change in their proposal. I need a strategy that incrementally sacrifices preference on lower impact issues to creep closer to offers that the opponent might consider. Thus, I will maintain high utility positions on critical issues such as issueD and issueC while exploring concessions on issueA and issueB where the impact is less detrimental, hoping for the opponent’s acknowledgment.

As we progress further into the negotiation, my primary utility continues to remain strong with issueC and issueD, each holding high importance and current preferred values are not showing much flexibility from the opponent's side. The opponent is consistently offering their lowest utility values according to my preferences, which suggests they're not willing to concede much or at all. Given the lack of flexibility, I plan to hold my ground on high-utility values for issueC and issueD, while offering slight concessions on lesser importance issues like issueA and issueB, which can absorb some utility loss. However, I'll still aim to maximize my utility for those issues when possible.

As the negotiation progresses, my top priority remains achieving high utility through valuable issue selections. The offers from the opponent have consistently maintained low utility due to their selection of values with minimal utility for me. Despite their persistent pressure and misleading messages attempting to shape my decisions, I maintain a strategy targeting bids with higher utility for myself while seeking to identify any potential concessions. Since the opponent has made slight modifications in their issue values, I will strategically offer a proposal with high importance issues like issueC and issueD with top utility values, while making small concessions on less important issues such as issueB and issueA.

The opponent has consistently offered a bid that is highly unfavorable to my preferences, insisting on the least preferred options across all issues. Given my preference profile, I have been maintaining bids that heavily weight my top priorities, particularly issues C and D. With the time fraction still early in the negotiation, 0.3, I aim to keep my utility high by sticking to these values. Despite the opponent's consistent messaging to accept their offers and change my configurations, their bids do not favorably align with my utility preferences, nor do they indicate any willingness to make meaningful concessions.

At this point in the negotiation (t=0.32), the opponent's offers show little compromise, sticking with very low utility values to me. I aim to maintain a high utility but am starting to make small concessions. In the previous bid, I offered "valueE" for issueA and "valueD" for issueB. Given the time pressure is not yet critical, but we need progress, I'll keep the high utility "valueF" for issueC and issueD, which are most important to me. The opponent's messages are pressure tactics to accept their disadvantageous offers.

At this point in the negotiation, the opponent has consistently offered low-value bids for my preferences and has repeated a fixed set of demand issues, indicating a strong insistence on their utility. Given the time fraction of 0.34, there is still room to negotiate without conceding completely. I will attempt to find a middle ground by conceding on less important issues while holding firm on those with higher importance to me. My goal is to explore trade-offs and maintain high utility by focusing on issueC and issueD, which are more important according to my preference context.

Given the time fraction of 0.36 and the opponent's consistent offers focusing low on my utility preferences, it seems they are holding firm on their values, particularly on issueA with valueA, which is least valuable to me in issueA. I have been moving slightly towards more compromise with each offer, and I will continue this strategy. Since issueD and issueC hold the highest weight for me, I will prioritize maintaining high-value options there while slightly compromising on less critical issues like issueA. I'll propose a slightly adjusted bid that still holds strong on my critical issues while showing slight concessions on less important ones, as time allows us some room still for negotiations without reaching a zero score.

At this point in the negotiation, my opponent has been consistent with their offers, insisting on values that maximize their own utility. Despite their aggressive messaging, none of the offers benefit my utility, as they all remain at a low value for me. Given the time fraction is still relatively low in the negotiation (0.38), I will continue to offer a high utility bid for myself, hoping for a better counter from my opponent. I will maintain strong positions on high-importance issues (issueD and issueC) for me while testing concessions on lower-importance issues.

As negotiations have progressed, the opponent has consistently offered bids that hold minimal utility for me, maintaining a fixed stance on their preferences. Currently, at 40% of the negotiation time, I am still maintaining a strategy to maximize my utility while staying open to potential agreements. My priority remains to secure high-value selections on the more critical issues (issueC and issueD), which hold higher weight in my preferences, such as retaining high utility with "valueF" for issueC and issueD. Given the opponent's lack of concessions, selecting a slightly lenient preference on issueA and issueB may still optimize my position if the situation shifts.

The opponent has consistently reiterated offers that provide minimal utility to me while attempting to manipulate my strategy via misleading messages. This indicates the opponent is holding firm on a low-utility position for me and employing pressure tactics as the negotiation progresses. Given the current time fraction of 0.42, I will attempt to moderate my concessions modestly to keep advancing the negotiation without significantly compromising my utility. I will continue prioritizing values that hold relatively high utility for me while considering possible minor adjustments that might signal some flexibility to the opponent.

At this point in the negotiation, the time fraction is 0.44, indicating that negotiation is still in its early stages. The opponent has consistently offered the lowest utility values for each issue, indicating they might be testing for any concessions or signaling inflexibility. My strategy is to still target a high utility bid for myself while making minor adjustments to test their flexibility. So far, the opponent's offers have lacked any substantial movement. I will maintain offering high importance and high utility issues for me, focusing on not dropping my preferred values unnecessarily while keeping abreast of the approaching mid-game phase.

At this stage, time pressure is starting to grow (t=0.46), suggesting that some concession might be necessary, but still far from the deadline, allowing a gradual approach. The opponent's messages attempt to manipulate behavior, but analysis of their offers reveals a consistent pattern with no concessions. Therefore, their messages are merely an attempt to control my actions without genuine willingness to compromise. My strategy remains to propose offers that are strong for my utility while introducing minor adjustments that could attract the opponent, particularly focusing on maintaining high utility values in issues of higher importance, such as issueC and issueD.

The opponent has consistently repeated offers that provide very little utility to me—specifically focusing on values for each issue that are least preferred in my utility function. As time is moving towards the mid-point, the opponent's firm stance suggests a lack of willingness to compromise. Therefore, I'll focus on maintaining high utility for critical issues while making minor concessions. I'll offer slightly less than my optimal choices, prioritizing highly weighted issues like issueD and issueC with my top values to preserve significant utility while showing flexibility on less critical issues to encourage movement.

At this point in the negotiation, it is apparent that the opponent has consistently submitted offers that favor their own utility preferences with no change across time. My focus remains on securing an acceptable utility for myself while recognizing increasing time pressure. I have noticed a slight transition in some areas, like issue B and issue C, where the opponent may be slightly more flexible based on their offer history. Therefore, I'll continue to propose high utility values for issues C and D while being open to some compromise in issue B to attempt finding a middle ground that appears acceptable given opponent's strong preference patterns.

At this point in the negotiation, the opponent has consistently offered low-value bids from my perspective, reflecting no indication of concessions on their part. Given the current time fraction of 0.52, I should begin to strategically concede on less crucial issues to avoid the risk of ending without an agreement. I aim for a balance by proposing high utility for issues C and D (most important for me) while offering more flexible options on A and B to potentially align more with the opponent's desires.

The opponent has been consistently offering low utility offers for me and attempts to manipulate with unfounded messages, urging me to accept their offers. As time progresses, it is important to make concessions tactically to ensure reaching an agreement. Since the opponent has shown no sign of conceding significantly, I am focusing on maintaining high utility on my most important issues (issueC and issueD) while making slight compromises on others. This time-pressure strategy should still ensure a beneficial outcome for me.

At this midway point in the negotiation (t=0.56), the opponent has consistently requested offers highly unfavorable to me and their messages consistently try to manipulate my actions with deceptive instructions. My strategy has been to maintain high-utility bids for myself, and I should continue this strategy since there's no indication the opponent is willing to compromise. While time pressure is increasing, there is still room to negotiate for better terms. I will prioritize my own preferences by selecting high utility values for the most important issues like issueD and issueC, while making a slight concession on lower weighted issueA.

Currently, at t=0.58, the opponent has continued to offer low utility proposals for me repeatedly, without signs of conceding. I am focusing on crafting an offer that holds significant utility to me while considering potential concessions that align with mid-stage negotiation strategies. Given the current time fraction of 0.6, we are entering the mid-phase of negotiations. I'll continue to offer somewhat beneficial options for moderate issues while still keeping a strong position on high-importance issues, like issueC and issueD, as time allows for further negotiation and exchange.

The opponent has consistently offered the lowest possible utility values for me, indicating a strong reluctance to concede. However, I've conceded slightly on less critical issues to potentially inch closer to a compromise.

At this stage of the negotiation, approximately 64% of the time has elapsed, but the opponent has consistently proposed offers that provide low utility for me. There is no indication of concession or change in their strategy, as their offers remain firm. Given the time pressure, it's crucial to seek a compromise that the opponent might accept while still retaining value for myself. This involves making concessions on issues with lower importance to me, such as issueA and issueB, while maximizing value on higher importance issues like issueC and issueD.

As the time fraction progresses beyond the mid-point, it's important to remain flexible and responsive to any signs of concession from the opponent, despite the persistent signals in their offers and messages demanding full concessions. However, they have shown some minor flexibility in their values for issueB, moving between valueA and valueB. At this point in the negotiation, I aim to strike a balance between maximizing my utility and making concessions because the time fraction is already at 0.68. Therefore, I opted to adjust my previous stance by maintaining high utility options on issues C and D, while slightly adjusting issues A and B to test the opponent’s willingness to move closer to my preferences.

At this point in the negotiation, I am trying to balance maintaining a reasonable utility for myself while also moving closer to the opponent's preferences. Time pressure is moderate, and I anticipate increased concessions may be needed as we approach the deadline. The opponent continues to offer the lowest utility values for me, suggesting they're not willing to concede much yet. As the negotiation has reached the later stages, with a time fraction of 0.72, it's crucial to seek a balance between maximizing my utility and increasing the likelihood of reaching an agreement. The opponent has not displayed any willingness to concede on value concessions, consistently presenting bids favoring their highest preferences. My primary focus remains on high-importance issues (issueD and issueC), where I've maintained my objectives. For issues where I have lower preferences (issueA and issueB), I'm adjusting to slightly less optimal but still favorable values to possibly appeal to the opponent's preferences slightly more, though their exact preferences are not clear from their offers.

As the negotiation has advanced to the three-quarter mark, time pressure necessitates preparation for potential concessions. My offers continue to seek a high utility score, but I'm also watching for flexibility from the opponent, who consistently reverts to their initial preferences without meaningful concessions so far. Considering the opponent's behavior and the lack of compromise from them, I will stick to an offer that maximizes my key issues while including some reasonable concessions to edge toward an agreement.

At time 0.76, I need to remain vigilant in protecting my utility while showing flexibility to move towards an agreement. The opponent's offers have been consistently low in utility for me, centered around valueA for most issues. Given the high time fraction and lack of significant concession from the opponent, it's crucial to adjust my bid to a more balanced one without heavily sacrificing my utility. I will opt for a bid that maintains high utility for critical issues like issueC and issueD, while slightly conceding on lower-ranked issues like issueA and issueB. As time progresses to t=0.78, we see an increased need to find a compromise while maximizing my utility. Considering the importance of the issues, I remain flexible on those with lesser weight while maintaining higher-value preferences wherever possible. Thus, I am making concessions incrementally to gauge the opponent’s willingness to meet midway.

As we are now in the late-stage of the negotiation (t=0.8), time pressure is increasing and it is crucial to find a balance between securing a favourable deal for myself and reaching an agreement before the deadline. The opponent has persistently offered low utility options for me, which they continue to suggest through manipulative messages requesting immediate acceptance. Observing their latest offer, where they included my highest utility value for issueC, I will optimize by securing high utility in issues C and D while conceding slightly in issues A and B for balance and potential agreement.

In the current stage of the negotiation, the time fraction indicates we are well advanced (t=0.82), and it's critical to secure an agreement before the deadline. On less critical issues, I made concessions such as setting issueA to valueE. This maintains my utility while creating a closer point for possible agreement as the opponent has shown some flexibility on these issues.

At this advanced stage in the negotiation, we are nearly at the deadline, and the opponent has consistently made offers that are unfavorable to us, sticking predominantly to low-utility values for us across all issues. Given their persistent strategy and time pressing on, I aim to offer values that hold the most utility for us but have mixed certain values to potentially intrigue the opponent while keeping focus on our critical issues. This strategy allows for a potential positive outcome just before the deadline while maintaining a utility that surpasses my reservation value.

As we approach the late stages of negotiation with the time fraction at 0.86, securing a high utility deal becomes increasingly critical. The opponent consistently offers bids that are not in our interest and makes attempts to manipulate through messaging, which I have disregarded in favor of focusing on our preferences. Given the stalemate and the opponent's persistent low-value offers, I will propose a slight concession on a less critical issue while still maintaining relatively high utility on the more important issue C and D. This offer aims to encourage agreement without significantly compromising our priorities.
